Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking magenta squares moves them 1 step diagonally toward the blue creature. They stay magenta. White bar shrinks by 2 per action (now at 42).
- Goal model: Move all dots to the blue creature. Need to find a more efficient way.
- Action model: Clicking dots moves them toward creature. Need to test other interactions.
- Plan: Try clicking on the blue creature itself to see if it does something different.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 12 of about 42 target actions so far.

sections.content: Let me check what changed after clicking on the blue creature.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after clicking blue creature
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Objects on main board:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['boundary'][0][0] > 10 and n['color'] not in ['B', 'W']:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary'][:4]}")

# Check white bar
bar = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'W']
print(f"\nWhite bar pixels: {bar[0]['pixels'] if bar else 'N/A'}")

# Check blue creature
blue = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b']
print(f"\nBlue creature: pixels={blue[0]['pixels'] if blue else 'N/A'}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking on the blue creature didn't change the dots' positions - they're still at the same places. The white bar went from 42 to 40 (lost 2 pixels as expected). So clicking on the creature doesn't do anything special for the dots.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics. The dots are moving toward the blue creature when clicked. Maybe I need to keep clicking them until they reach the creature. But with 4 dots at various distances, this could take many actions.
Let me calculate distances:
- (38, 35) to creature center (~26, 34): ~12 steps up, ~1 step left = ~12 clicks
- (39, 14) to creature center: ~13 steps up, ~20 steps right = ~20 clicks
- (54, 45) to creature center: ~28 steps up, ~11 steps left = ~28 clicks
- (55, 14) to creature center: ~29 steps up, ~20 steps right = ~29 clicks
Total: ~89 clicks to move all dots to the creature. That's way too many given the white bar constraint.
Wait, maybe the game isn't about moving dots to the creature. Let me reconsider.
Looking at the top panel: S, M, p, Y (sky blue, magenta, purple, yellow). The dots started as sky blue, became magenta when clicked. Maybe I need to click on the colored squares in the top panel to select a color, then click on dots to paint them that color?
Or maybe the goal is different. Let me look at the yellow square on the top bar. It's at (3, 30) with 16 pixels (4x4). Maybe clicking on it does something.
Actually, let me think about this differently. The white bar is shrinking by 2 per action. If it started at 64 and needs to reach some target, maybe the goal is to make it reach 0 or some specific value by performing the right actions.
Or maybe I should try ACTION7 to see what it does.
Actually, let me try clicking on the colored squares in the top panel to see if that changes something about the dots.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking the blue creature doesn't affect the dots. Dots stay in place. White bar shrinks by 2/action. Need to understand the real goal.
Goal model: Unclear. Maybe need to interact with top panel colors or use ACTION7.
Plan: Try clicking on the colored squares in the top panel to see if they affect the dots.
